verses.flow: Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the assembly of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ:
Grace to you and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
We are obligated to give thanks to God always concerning you, brothers, even as it is worthy, because your faith grows exceedingly and the love of each one of all of you abounds toward one another,
verses.note: GREEK: "ὑπεραυξάνει" (hyperauxanei) is a NT hapax legomenon (Strong's G5232), an intensified compound of "auxanō" (to grow), with the prefix "hyper-" indicating extraordinary, beyond-normal growth. Paul coins or employs this rare term to emphasize that Thessalonian faith is not merely growing but surging beyond expectation. "πλεονάζει" (pleonazei, G4121) carries the sense of overflowing abundance, reinforcing the same intensification applied to their mutual love.

verses.flow: so that we ourselves boast in you among the assemblies of God, on behalf of your endurance and faith in all your persecutions and the afflictions which you bear,
which is a manifest token of the righteous judgment of God, unto your being counted worthy of the kingdom of God, on behalf of which also you suffer,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "ἔνδειγμα" (endeigma, G1730) is another NT hapax legomenon, meaning a manifest proof or plain evidence. Paul argues that the Thessalonians' patient endurance under persecution is itself the visible proof of God's righteous judgment — not that suffering is punitive, but that God's just verdict is already at work, refining and counting them worthy of the kingdom (cf. Phil 1:28). TYPOLOGY: The concept of suffering as evidence of divine vindication echoes the righteous sufferer tradition of the Psalms (Ps 34:19) and anticipates the eschatological reversal when God sets all things right.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: since indeed it is a righteous thing with God to repay affliction to those who afflict you,

words.m: verb present passive participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: and to you who are afflicted, relief with us, at the revelation of the Lord Jesus from heaven with the angels of his power,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "μετ' ἀγγέλων δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ" (with the angels of his power) depicts Christ's parousia as a royal-divine theophany. The Lord Jesus arrives from heaven accompanied by his heavenly host — the angelic members of the divine council serving as agents of his sovereign might. This imagery draws directly on OT theophanies where YHWH comes with his holy myriads (Deut 33:2; Dan 7:10; Zech 14:5) and applies them without qualification to Christ, identifying him as the enthroned divine King executing cosmic judgment. GREEK: "ἀποκαλύψει" (apokalypsei, G602) denotes an unveiling or uncovering — the removal of the veil between heaven and earth when Christ's hidden glory becomes visible to all.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb perfect active participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: in flaming fire, rendering vengeance to those who do not know God and to those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus,
who shall pay the penalty of eternal destruction away from the face of the Lord and from the glory of his might,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "ὄλεθρον αἰώνιον" (olethron aiōnion) — eternal destruction — denotes not annihilation but an ongoing ruin characterized by separation "ἀπὸ προσώπου τοῦ κυρίου" (from the face of the Lord). The punishment is defined relationally: banishment from the Lord's presence and from the glory of his strength. TYPOLOGY: The language of being cast from the divine presence echoes Isaiah 2:10, 19, 21 (LXX), where the terrified hide from the face of the Lord and the splendor of his majesty — Paul applies this Isaianic Day-of-YHWH imagery directly to Christ.

words.m: verb aorist active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: when he comes to be glorified in his holy ones and to be marveled at among all those who believed, because our testimony to you was believed, in that day.
Unto which end also we pray always concerning you, that our God may count you worthy of the calling, and may fulfill every good pleasure of goodness and every work of faith in power,
so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν καὶ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ" may be read under Granville Sharp's rule as identifying one referent — "our God and Lord Jesus Christ" — though most translations preserve two referents. Either reading places Christ in the closest possible relationship with God as the single source of grace. STRUCTURE: This verse forms a chiastic climax: the name of Jesus glorified in believers, and believers glorified in him — a mutual indwelling of glory that echoes the high-priestly prayer of John 17:21-22 and serves as the telos toward which Paul's entire prayer in vv. 11-12 is directed.

verses.flow: Now we ask you, brothers, concerning the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and our gathering together unto Him,
verses.note: GREEK: "Parousia" (παρουσία, Strong's G3952) denotes a royal "arrival" or "presence," used as a technical term for Christ's return in glory. "Episynagōgē" (ἐπισυναγωγή, G1997) appears only here and in Heb 10:25, referring to the eschatological gathering of believers to Christ. TYPOLOGY: The gathering echoes the OT hope of Israel's regathering (Isa 11:12; Deut 30:3-4), now fulfilled in the assembly of all saints unto the risen Messiah.

verses.flow: that you not be quickly shaken from your mind, nor be troubled, neither through a spirit, nor through a word, nor through a letter as if from us, as though the day of the Lord has come.
verses.note: GREEK: "Saleuō" (σαλεύω, G4531) means to shake or agitate, as a ship tossed by waves — conveying mental instability. "Enestēken" (ἐνέστηκεν) is perfect tense of "enistēmi," meaning "has come" or "is now present," indicating some in Thessalonica believed the Day of the Lord had already arrived. HISTORICAL: False letters circulating under Paul's name were a known problem in the early church, prompting his authenticating signature mark (cf. 2 Thess 3:17).

verses.flow: Let no one deceive you in any manner, for that day will not come unless the falling away comes first, and the man of lawlessness is revealed, the son of destruction,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostasia" (ἀποστασία, G646) denotes a departure or defection — a standing away from. In the LXX it carries overtones of covenantal rebellion (Josh 22:22). "Ho anthrōpos tēs anomias" — "the man of lawlessness" — echoes Daniel's little horn and the abomination language. THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes a necessary sequence: the great apostasy and the revelation of the lawless one must precede the Day of the Lord, countering an over-realized eschatology.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: the one opposing and exalting himself above every one called god or object of worship, so that he sits in the temple of God, displaying himself that he is god.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Every one called god" (πάντα λεγόμενον θεόν) likely encompasses both the true God and the so-called gods of the nations (cf. 1 Cor 8:5), reflecting a divine council awareness where spiritual powers bear the title "theos." The lawless one's act of enthronement in the "naos" (inner sanctuary) is the ultimate usurpation of divine prerogative. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrating the temple (Dan 11:31) and Jesus's reference to the abomination of desolation standing in the holy place (Matt 24:15).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Do you not remember that while I was still with you, I was telling you these things?

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And now you know that which restrains, for him to be revealed in his own time.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the mystery of lawlessness is already at work; only the one who now restrains will do so until he is taken out of the midst.
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystērion" (μυστήριον, G3466) denotes not a puzzle but a divine secret now partially disclosed. "Ho katechōn" (the one restraining, masculine) shifts from the neuter "to katechon" in v6, suggesting both a principle and a personal agent of restraint. THEOLOGICAL: The identity of the restrainer remains one of the most debated questions in Pauline eschatology — proposed identifications include the Holy Spirit, the Roman imperial order, the archangel Michael (Dan 12:1), or the proclamation of the gospel itself.

verses.flow: And then the lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will slay with the breath of His mouth and will bring to nothing by the appearing of His coming,
verses.note: GREEK: "Anelei" (ἀνελεῖ) means "will slay" or "will destroy" — the Lord Jesus dispatches the lawless one merely with the breath (pneuma) of His mouth. "Epiphaneia" (ἐπιφάνεια, G2015) means a brilliant, visible manifestation — in Hellenistic usage it described the visible appearance of a deity. TYPOLOGY: The breath-of-mouth destruction echoes Isaiah 11:4 (LXX), where the Messianic root of Jesse slays the wicked with the breath of His lips, identifying Jesus directly with the Davidic warrior-king.

verses.flow: whose coming is according to the working of Satan, with all power and signs and wonders of falsehood,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The lawless one's coming is a dark parody of Christ's parousia — "kata energeian tou Satana" (according to the working of Satan). The triad of "power, signs, and wonders" mimics the authenticating marks of apostolic ministry (2 Cor 12:12; Acts 2:22), but qualified by "pseudous" (of falsehood), indicating counterfeit supernatural activity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan operates as the adversary within the spiritual realm, empowering a human agent in imitation of how the Spirit empowers Christ's servants.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: and with all deceit of unrighteousness for those who are perishing, because they did not receive the love of the truth so as to be saved.
And for this reason God sends upon them a working of delusion, so that they should believe the lie,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Energeian planēs" — God sends a "working of delusion." This is judicial hardening: because they refused truth, God gives them over to deception (cf. Rom 1:24-28; 1 Kgs 22:19-23). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The passage echoes the divine council scene of 1 Kings 22 where a deceiving spirit is sent with God's sovereign permission. God remains sovereign even over the operation of deception, using it to effect judgment upon those who have already chosen unrighteousness.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: in order that all may be judged who did not believe the truth but took pleasure in unrighteousness.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle plural masculine

verses.flow: But we are bound to give thanks to God always concerning you, brothers beloved by the Lord, because God chose you as a firstfruit unto salvation, in sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth,
verses.note: GREEK: "Aparchēn" (ἀπαρχήν, G536) — "firstfruit" — is the preferred reading over "ap' archēs" (from the beginning), though manuscripts vary. As firstfruits, the Thessalonians represent the initial harvest consecrated to God. "Hagiasmō pneumatos" — sanctification wrought by the Spirit — paired with "pistei alētheias" — faith in the truth — presents the divine and human dimensions of salvation. TYPOLOGY: The firstfruits language connects to the Feast of Firstfruits (Lev 23:10) and Christ as the firstfruits of resurrection (1 Cor 15:20).

verses.flow: unto which He called you through our gospel, unto the obtaining of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ.
So then, brothers, stand firm and hold fast the traditions which you were taught, whether through word or through our letter.
verses.note: GREEK: "Paradoseis" (παραδόσεις, G3862) — "traditions" or "things handed over" — used positively here for apostolic teaching transmitted orally and in writing. This same word is used negatively for human traditions that nullify God's word (Mark 7:8-9), but here it refers to authoritative apostolic instruction. THEOLOGICAL: Paul grounds ecclesial stability in faithful reception of apostolic teaching, establishing the principle that both spoken and written apostolic witness carry equal authority.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now may our Lord Jesus Christ Himself, and God our Father, the One who loved us and gave eternal comfort and good hope by grace,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 16-17 form a single prayer-wish (optative mood in v17). Notably, "our Lord Jesus Christ" is named before "God our Father," and the singular participles "ho agapēsas" (the one who loved) and "dous" (having given) grammatically coordinate both persons under a single action, reflecting the unity of the Father and Son in gracious operation toward believers. THEOLOGICAL: The eternal nature of the comfort (paraklēsin aiōnian) points beyond temporal encouragement to eschatological hope grounded in grace (en chariti).

verses.flow: comfort your hearts and establish you in every good work and word.
As for the rest, pray, brothers, concerning us, that the word of the Lord may run and be glorified, even as also with you,
verses.note: GREEK: The verb τρέχῃ (trechō, G5143) means "to run" and is used metaphorically for the rapid and unhindered spread of the gospel message. The coupling with δοξάζηται ("be glorified") portrays the word of the Lord as a living force that advances and is honored wherever it goes. This echoes Psalm 147:15 (LXX) where God's word runs swiftly.

verses.flow: and that we may be delivered from perverse and evil men; for the faith is not of all.
But faithful is the Lord, who shall establish you and guard you from the evil one.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul employs a wordplay between πίστις ("faith") in v.2 and πιστός ("faithful") here—though not all men possess faith, the Lord is unfailingly faithful. The phrase ἀπὸ τοῦ πονηροῦ ("from the evil one") is articular, likely denoting Satan personally rather than evil in the abstract (cf. Matt 6:13; John 17:15). This assurance of divine protection directly follows the acknowledgment of spiritual opposition.

verses.flow: And we have confidence in the Lord concerning you, that the things which we command, you both do and shall do.
And may the Lord direct your hearts into the love of God and into the steadfast endurance of Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The optative κατευθύναι (kateuthynai, G2720) expresses a prayer-wish: "may He direct." The phrase εἰς τὴν ὑπομονὴν τοῦ Χριστοῦ can be rendered "the steadfast endurance of Christ," meaning either the endurance that Christ exemplified or the patient waiting for Christ's return. Given the eschatological context of this letter (ch. 2), both senses likely resonate. TYPOLOGY: The directing of hearts echoes Proverbs 3:6 (LXX), where the Lord makes straight one's paths.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to withdraw yourselves from every brother walking disorderly and not according to the tradition which they received from us.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb στέλλεσθαι (stellesthai, G4724) means "to withdraw, shrink back, keep away from" and has a nautical background of furling sails. The term ἀτάκτως (ataktōs, G814) means "disorderly" and derives from military language for a soldier breaking rank. HISTORICAL: The παράδοσις ("tradition") here refers to apostolic teaching transmitted orally and by example during Paul's time in Thessalonica, not later ecclesiastical tradition.

verses.flow: For you yourselves know how it is necessary to imitate us, because we did not behave disorderly among you,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: nor did we eat bread from anyone without cost, but in labor and toil, night and day working, so as not to be a burden to any of you—
not because we do not have authority, but that we might give ourselves as a pattern to you, for you to imitate us.
verses.note: GREEK: The word τύπον (typon, G5179) means "pattern, model, example"—literally a mark left by a blow, hence an imprint to be replicated. Paul claims ἐξουσίαν ("authority," G1849) to receive material support from the churches (cf. 1 Cor 9:4-14), yet voluntarily relinquished it to establish a behavioral paradigm for the congregation. TYPOLOGY: This self-denial for the sake of others mirrors Christ's own pattern of kenosis (Phil 2:5-8).

verses.flow: For even when we were with you, this we commanded you: that if anyone is not willing to work, neither let him eat.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we hear that some are walking among you disorderly, working at nothing, but being busybodies.
verses.note: GREEK: Paul employs a masterful paronomasia: μηδὲν ἐργαζομένους ἀλλὰ περιεργαζομένους—"working at nothing but being busybodies." The prefix περι- transforms the verb from "to work" (ergazomai, G2038) to "to work around, meddle" (periergazomai, G4020, a NT hapax legomenon). The wordplay is untranslatable in its full force but underscores the ironic inversion: those who refuse productive labor become occupied with meddling in others' affairs.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Now such ones we command and exhort in the Lord Jesus Christ, that with quietness working, they eat their own bread.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But you, brothers, do not grow weary in doing good.
And if anyone does not obey our word through this letter, take note of this one, and do not associate with him, that he may be put to shame.
And do not regard him as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother.
Now may the Lord of peace Himself give you peace continually in every way. The Lord be with you all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The title ὁ κύριος τῆς εἰρήνης ("the Lord of peace") parallels the Hebrew divine title found in Judges 6:24 (YHWH Shalom). Paul applies this title to Christ, who is the source and sovereign over peace. The phrase διὰ παντὸς ἐν παντὶ τρόπῳ ("continually in every way") emphasizes the comprehensive and unceasing nature of this divine peace. The closing benediction "the Lord be with you all" echoes covenantal blessing language (Ruth 2:4; cf. Matt 28:20).

verses.flow: The greeting by my own hand—of Paul—which is a sign in every letter; thus I write.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Paul typically dictated his letters through an amanuensis (cf. Rom 16:22) but would add a closing greeting in his own handwriting as authentication. The fact that he explicitly calls this a σημεῖον ("sign, distinguishing mark," G4592) in every letter suggests concern about forged correspondence—likely prompted by the possible pseudonymous letter referenced in 2 Thess 2:2 that had unsettled the congregation regarding the Day of the Lord.

verses.flow: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all.
